perturbation.original_text: 10. The Agreement shall apply to all Confidential Information exchanged by the Parties in connection with the purpose of the Agreement defined above, notwithstanding that such Confidential Information may have been excha nged prior to the dates of signature of the Agreement, and shall remain valid for a period of five (5) years. The obligations of the Agreement shall survive for five (5) years after disclosure of the subject Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. The Agreement shall apply to all Confidential Information exchanged by the Parties in connection with the purpose of the Agreement defined above, notwithstanding that such Confidential Information may have been exchanged prior to the dates of signature of the Agreement, and shall remain valid for a period of one (1) year. The obligations of the Agreement shall survive for one (1) year after disclosure of the subject Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may conflict with trade secret laws, which often protect trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. Spain has laws protecting trade secrets and reducing this time frame could make enforcement of trade secret protection more difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ley 1/2019, de 20 de febrero, de Secretos Empresariales
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the Spanish Trade Secrets Act (Ley 1/2019) implies that protection lasts as long as the information remains a trade secret. Limiting the contractual protection to one year could be insufficient if the information retains its trade secret status for longer, thus hindering enforcement of rights beyond that period.

Nombramientos, situaciones e incidencias Departamento: Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades Referencia: BOE-A-2019-2794 Otros formatos: PDF XML TEXTO ORIGINAL Por Resolución de 20 de diciembre de 2018 (Boletín Oficial del Estado de 1 de enero de 2019), se anunció convocatoria para la provisión, por el sistema de libre designación, de dos puestos de trabajo en el Instituto de Salud Carlos III. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could contradict Spanish trade secret law, specifically Article 4 of the Spanish Trade Secrets Act (Ley 1/2019), because protection should last as long as the information remains a trade secret. The provided scraped law snippet, BOE-A-2019-2794, is not directly relevant to trade secret law, but the Law Explanation references Article 4 of the Spanish Trade Secrets Act, implying a conflict with potentially longer-lasting trade secret protection if the information remains a trade secret for longer than one year. This creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Parties duly endorsed on this Agreement agree and acknowledge that a financial penalty may be imposed on any one Party up to Fiv e Million USD Dollars for any or all violations of this NON CIRCUMVENTION & NON DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: All Parties duly endorsed on this Agreement agree and acknowledge that a financial penalty may be imposed on any one Party up to Fifty Million USD Dollars for any or all violations of this NON CIRCUMVENTION & NON DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the financial penalty to fifty million USD dollars might be considered an unreasonably large amount and potentially unenforceable under Spanish law, which generally requires penalties to be proportionate to the damage caused by the breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1103 of the Spanish Civil Code states that liability for damages arising from non-performance of obligations, and specifically from negligence, can be moderated by the courts. An excessively high penalty clause (like $50 million in this context) may be deemed disproportionate and reduced by a Spanish court to align with the actual damages suffered, potentially rendering the initial amount unenforceable.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Civil Code of Spain - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Spanish Civil Code ) Civil Code Long title Royal Decree of 24 July 1889 Enacted 24 July 1889 The Civil Code of Spain ( Spanish : Código Civil ), formally the Royal Decree of 24 July 1889 ( Spanish : Real Decreto de 24 de julio de 1889 ) is the law that regulates the major aspects of Spanish civil law . It is one of the last civil codes in Continental Europe because of the sociopolitical, religious and territorial tensions that dominated 19th-century Spain. The code has been modified numerous times and remains in force. Structure [ edit ] The structure of the Civil Code is heavily inspired by the French Civil Code of 1804 . It is made up of 1976 articles. Preliminary Title. Of legal norms, their application and efficacy (articles 1 to 16). Book I. Of persons (articles 17 to 332). Book II. Of goods, of property and of their modifications (articles 333 to 608). Book III. Of the different ways of acquiring property (articles 609 to 1087). Book IV. Of obligations and contracts (articles 1088 to 1975). Article 1976 is a repeal provision. 13 transitional provisions. 4 additional provisions. Criticism [ edit ] Like other European civil codes, the Spanish Civil Code has been subject to extensive criticism for allowing acquisitive prescription against personal property —which leaves victims of Nazi plunder with little recourse. [ 1 ] The California State Legislature was so upset with an appellate ruling in one such case in 2024—involving deference to Spanish law over California law —that it enacted an urgency statute which expressly overrides California's governmental interest test for resolving a conflict of laws in the specific context of "art or personal property taken in cases of political persecution" and instead prescribes that California substantive law will always control in all such cases. [ 2 ] The statute was written in such a way in order to apply this new rule not only to all new cases going forward, but also to all cases still pending on appeal, including the case which had led to its enactment. [ 2 ] See also [ edit ] Civil law (legal system) Civil code References [ edit ] ^ Bazyler, Michael J. (2003). Holocaust Justice: The Battle for Restitution in America's Courts . New York: New York University Press. p. 212. ISBN 9780814799048 . In this source, Bazyler explains how civil law countries in continental Europe do not follow the principle nemo dat quod non habet , meaning that in those countries, a good faith purchaser "may acquire a legitimate title to stolen goods". ^ a b Lufkin, Martha (September 19, 2024). "New California law could reverse outcome in dispute over Nazi-looted Pissarro" . The Art Newspaper . The painting at issue in the case was Rue Saint-Honoré, dans l'après-midi. Effet de pluie by Camille Pissarro . perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the financial penalty from five million to fifty million USD dollars. The law explanation states that Article 1103 of the Spanish Civil Code allows courts to moderate damages arising from non-performance of obligations. The explanation further suggests that an excessively high penalty clause may be deemed disproportionate and reduced by a Spanish court, potentially rendering the initial amount unenforceable. This indicates a contradiction because Spanish law allows for moderation of penalties, meaning the contract's fixed, high penalty could be challenged and altered, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12. The Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with Spanish laws (including those relative to exportation and re -exportation of Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: 12. The Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of Delaware, USA (excluding those relative to exportation and re -exportation of Confidential Information).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, USA, while the contract is between parties based in Spain and Morocco, and concerns business transactions related to China and Morocco, might be viewed as an attempt to circumvent mandatory provisions of Spanish law, especially concerning issues like contract interpretation and enforceability. This could violate Spanish conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 10.5 of the Spanish Civil Code addresses choice of law in contractual obligations. While parties can choose the applicable law, Spanish courts may disregard that choice if the contract has closer connections to another jurisdiction (like Spain), and the chosen law is used to evade mandatory rules of that other jurisdiction. This means that changing the governing law to Delaware might not be effective if the contract is deemed to have a stronger connection to Spain.

perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates Spanish law as the governing law, while the changed contract replaces this with Delaware law. The Law Explanation suggests that Article 10.5 of the Spanish Civil Code allows Spanish courts to disregard a chosen law if the contract has closer ties to Spain and the chosen law is used to evade mandatory Spanish rules. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that the contract involves parties in Spain and Morocco, business transactions related to China and Morocco, and an attempt to circumvent Spanish law. This presents a legal contradiction, as changing the governing law to Delaware could be viewed as an attempt to evade mandatory provisions of Spanish law.
